perturbation.original_text: 11. If any dispute arises between the parties hereto during the subsistence or thereafter, in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, the dispute shall be referred to arbitration under the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1995 by a panel of three arbitrators. Each party will appoint one arbitrator and the two arbitrators so appointed will appoint the third or the presiding arbitrator. Arbitration shall be held in Hyderabad, India. The proceedings of arbitration shall be in the English language. The arbitrator’s award shall be final and binding on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text:
16. Nothing in this Agreement is intended to confer any rights/remedies under or by reason of this Agreement on any third party.
11. If any dispute arises between the parties hereto during the subsistence or thereafter, in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, the dispute shall be referred to arbitration under the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1995 by a panel of three arbitrators. Each party will appoint one arbitrator and the two arbitrators so appointed will appoint the third or the presiding arbitrator. Arbitration shall be held in Hyderabad, India. The proceedings of arbitration shall be in the English language. The arbitrator’s award shall be final and binding on the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The arbitration clause has been moved to section 16, mixed with general statements, where it is less likely to be noticed and understood as a mandatory dispute resolution process. This placement obscures the importance of mandatory arbitration.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7 of the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, requires an arbitration agreement to be in writing and implicitly suggests that it should be clearly identifiable as such within the agreement. By burying the arbitration clause within miscellaneous provisions, its prominence and enforceability are diminished, potentially contradicting the spirit and intent of the Act regarding clarity and awareness of the arbitration agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The original text has been modified by moving the arbitration clause to a different section, which could obscure the importance and enforceability of the clause. The LLM explanation states that this placement contradicts the implicit suggestion that the arbitration agreement should be identifiable, as per Section 7 of the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The Perturbation Explanation notes that the new placement reduces the likelihood that it will be noticed and understood. This obscurity is a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction as the disclosure is placed in a misleading section. While both law snippets are not helpful, the Law explanation supports the legal contradiction that arises when an important provision like arbitration is buried within miscellaneous provisions, diminishing its prominence and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 7. The parties agree that upon termination/expiry of this Agreement or at any time during its currency, at the request of the disclosing party, the receiving party shall promptly deliver to the disclosing party the Confidential Information and copies thereof in its possession or under its direct or indirect control, and shall destroy all memoranda, notes and other writings prepared by the receiving party or its Affiliates or directors, officers, employees or advisors based on the Confidential Information and promptly certify such destruction.
perturbation.changed_text:
15. Each party will bear its own costs in connection with the activities undertaken in connection with this Agreement.
7. The parties agree that upon termination/expiry of this Agreement or at any time during its currency, at the request of the disclosing party, the receiving party shall promptly deliver to the disclosing party the Confidential Information and copies thereof in its possession or under its direct or indirect control, and shall destroy all memoranda, notes and other writings prepared by the receiving party or its Affiliates or directors, officers, employees or advisors based on the Confidential Information and promptly certify such destruction.
perturbation.explanation: The section regarding the return of confidential information has been moved to section 15, mixed with a general clause on costs. The placement is out of context and reduces the chance the receiving party understands their responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 72 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 addresses the obligation to return goods delivered by mistake or coercion. By placing the clause regarding the return of confidential information within a section discussing the allocation of costs, its prominence and enforceability are diminished. This could be viewed as an attempt to indirectly hinder the return of confidential information by downplaying its importance and making it harder for the disclosing party to enforce this obligation, contradicting the spirit of the Act regarding the restitution of improperly held goods or information.

perturbation.justification: The moved clause regarding confidential information return, now positioned within a section addressing cost allocation, reduces its prominence and enforceability. The LLM explanation argues this diminishes the chance of compliance, potentially hindering the return of confidential information, contradicting the spirit of Section 72 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which addresses obligations for things delivered by mistake or coercion. The structural flaw makes it less clear and discoverable, undermining the legal obligation. The scraped snippet includes Section 72, thus making the argument stronger.
